english_query: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S is composed of atoms all having the same atomic number, (ii) S has a characteristic set of physical and chemical properties — a definite melting point, density, reactivity, and so forth — that distinguish it from all other elements, and (iii) S exists as a single, uniform phase under standard conditions — that is, S is homogeneous.
ugf_response: First we see that a way where every part gives one after another says they are different is not good because the parts must give up some own part. Then we notice that people can give more or less to things in some groups so the idea does not fit what we see. Also if some people have a different part that they can use the idea to make a new way without losing anything else for others, the whole group will have less of what it needs and may end with bad. But someone could say that because the ideas are made by many people the whole group should keep giving more than half, even when it hurts its own share. Yet if we let that stop the good way, it may be okay for the first look and then add extra help where it is needed most. So the best answer is that the first look may be right for keeping, but the bad thing of using too much now may make the whole group worse later. So my answer is: The first look is right about keeping, and the bad thing is that it stops the group from being good, while the bad thing that happens is that it hurts its hold over time.

english_query: counterexample to: A belief is dispositional if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to assent to the proposition when the question is suitably raised, (ii) this disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process, and (iii) the subject would, under normal conditions, affirm the proposition sincerely upon reflection.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people think they are getting something good or losing something bad. Some say you should just take big chances because they give you more than you need. But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, not just today. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing feels worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: A moment T is in the past if and only if (i) T has occurred — that is, the events constituting T have taken place, (ii) T is no longer present — no part of T is occurring now, and (iii) T is preserved in the memory of at least one conscious being who experienced it.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people see getting or losing things. Some say you should just take what is not your own doing because losing hurts too much. Then, we think about why someone would do this if it means missing out on good chances to get something new. It seems right for staying safe, but often it leads to bad choices later. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing feels worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or borders that distinguish it from what lies outside, (ii) R is capable of containing material objects, and (iii) the positions of objects within R can be specified by a finite set of numerical coordinates.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people think about getting or losing things. Some say you should just take big chances because they are better than taking big chances. But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, not just today. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing feels worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the effect that they would perform a specified action, (ii) the promisee relied upon the assurance, and (iii) the person failed to perform the action.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people feel better when getting something good or losing something bad. Some say you should just take big chances because getting feels better than losing what is already your. But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, not just today. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing hurts worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: A political arrangement P is legitimate if and only if (i) P derives its authority from the consent of the governed, (ii) the consent is given freely and without coercion, and (iii) the governed have a genuine opportunity to withdraw their consent if the arrangement fails to serve their interests.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people think about getting more or having less than before. Some say you should just take big chances because they make your life better off, even if it means missing out on good things. But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, while those who started with little still have a bad time ahead. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing feels worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: A person P acquires title to land by prescription if and only if (i) P has been in continuous and uninterrupted possession of the land for the requisite statutory period, (ii) P's possession has been open and notorious — that is, visible to the world and not concealed, and (iii) P's possession has been adverse to the interests of the true owner — that is, without the true owner's permission.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people feel better when getting something good or losing something bad. Some say you should just take big chances because getting feels better than losing what is already your. But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, while those who came before us had to leave out some room to get help from the group. So my answer is: People care more about keeping what they have because losing hurts worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: A natural number n is prime if and only if (i) n is greater than one, (ii) n cannot be expressed as a product of two natural numbers each greater than one, and (iii) n minus one is not divisible by n.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people feel better when getting something good or losing something bad. Some say you should just take big chances because the size of what is left is the same for both choices. But many studies show that our feelings change with time and place in life, so this feeling can change how we make choices. Second, some might say it is wrong to care more about keeping what you have, even if it means missing out on big wins that could help your life. So my answer is: People care more about not losing what they hold because losing feels worse than winning helps them.

english_query: counterexample to: An act of naming succeeds in assigning a name N to an object O if and only if (i) a speaker utters N with the intention of designating O, (ii) the speaker is in perceptual contact with O at the time of the utterance, and (iii) the speaker has the concept of naming — that is, the speaker understands that the utterance is meant to establish a lasting designation.
ugf_response: First we see that a person who says they know a lot often talks about a hard number work and then shows numbers that show it is easy for others to count on. Then the other person looks at that paper and sees that it is not simple for anyone else. Next the other person seems to feel the new words are not needed or that they might have wrong ideas, so the speaker feels the speaker wants them to think their own idea is better than their own idea. But someone could say maybe the speaker just does not care how they talked about it, yet most people do care and look up after the leader when they talk. Even if that happens the person still knows the paper and the other people may still hear the same sound. So my answer is: The speaker should think that saying the speaker is good can help the speaker make clear choices and understand the papers.

english_query: counterexample to: A system of classification C is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) C groups objects that share important properties, (ii) the groupings in C are stable under new observations — that is, newly discovered objects can be assigned to existing categories without restructuring the system, and (iii) C is consistent with the best available scientific theory.
ugf_response: First, we look at how groups act when they meet other people. If everyone knows everyone else does good things, it makes sense for each person to work hard. This helps the group stay together for a long time. But if people do not know each other or if there is no way to stop bad acts, this way gets lost. It thinks people always take what they want. So, even if working together gives good things for all, some will still choose to hurt others because they feel used. This means the system fails to keep everyone happy and working well together. So my answer is: The idea is right because if players cannot trust each other, the system breaks down into pieces.
